briefing.opening: Hello everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for April 14, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Strengthening Domestic Climate Crisis Awareness and Education
sections.content: In today's briefing, we deliver a warning message about the intensifying climate crisis, alongside domestic and international efforts to address climate change. Various local governments, including Busanjin-gu, Wonju, and Seogwipo, are operating 'Climate Change Week,' hosting photo exhibitions and citizen participation events to raise awareness of the climate crisis [1, 2, 3, 10, 11, 23, 33]. Notably, a survey indicates that 57.5% of the public identify 'climate change' as the most urgent environmental issue, reflecting high public interest and concern regarding the climate crisis [7]. Anyang Green Maru operates a 'Carbon Neutrality Practice Sharing School' tailored for children, providing opportunities to learn about climate change through experiential activities [8, 35, 36, 37, 62, 98], and Gwangmyeong City and the Korean Red Cross are also strengthening climate education for youth [73, 102]. These efforts play a crucial role in enhancing overall societal understanding of climate change and strengthening the climate action capabilities of future generations [26].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic Efforts for Achieving Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Across the Republic of Korea, various efforts are actively underway to achieve carbon neutrality. Jeju Province is promoting a citizen-led culture of carbon neutrality with a target of 2035 [15, 20, 29, 67, 79], and Gyeongsangbuk-do is demonstrating its commitment to leading the carbon-neutral industry by fostering 100 climate tech companies [28, 41, 44, 47, 48, 65, 82]. Korea Southern Power (KOSPO) has raised its greenhouse gas reduction target to 72% by 2035, expressing a strong commitment to reduction [78]. The reduction efforts of 70 large domestic corporations, which account for 65% of domestic greenhouse gas emissions, are also being significantly addressed [24]. Gwangju City has established the nation's first tap water production system utilizing its own solar energy, presenting a carbon-neutral model [17, 49, 74, 96]. Carbon neutrality practices are expanding across daily life and policy, including the reduction of single-use items in funeral homes [54] and the nationwide spread of carbon-neutral linked volunteer activities [38, 51, 52, 60, 75, 81, 99, 101, 105]. Adaptation efforts in the agricultural sector, such as education on subtropical crops for climate change response [12, 30] and the premium branding of Yanggu apples [21], are also notable. The importance of implementing policies that consider the feasibility, cost structure, and public acceptance of greenhouse gas reduction is strongly emphasized [9].

sections.title: ⚡ Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Current Challenges
sections.content: Korea is establishing institutional foundations, such as the creation of a power regulatory agency and the revision of grid codes, to achieve its renewable energy target of 100GW by 2030 [110, 145, 147]. The nation's first public-led, resident-participatory RE100 floating solar power plant has begun operation at Hapcheon Dam [119, 132, 133, 135, 143], and the deployment of renewable energy is rapidly expanding, with Boryeong City, for example, mandating the installation of solar roofs on public parking lots [123, 129, 134, 142, 148, 149, 150]. Private sector investments are also active, including NH Nonghyup Bank's participation in the Shinan-Uii offshore wind project [137] and Hyundai Motor Group's expansion of renewable energy supply in India [111]. Synergies between technology and policy are being sought, with AI technology driving innovation in renewable energy development [141] and policies being implemented to encourage renewable energy consumption through electric vehicle charging fee discounts [122, 153]. However, to achieve the 100GW renewable energy target, criticisms are also raised, emphasizing the need to squarely face the reality of the distribution grid [108] and prioritizing procedures and securing public acceptance over a 'speed race' in renewable energy deployment [109]. Furthermore, challenges to be addressed include the controversy over the expansion of the Daejeon cogeneration plant [112], the necessity of a public transition to a hydrogen economy [121], and the dilemma of relying on specific technologies during the renewable energy transition [124].

sections.title: ⚠️ Intensification of the Global Climate Crisis and International Response
sections.content: Globally, warning signs of the intensifying climate crisis are becoming clearer. Last month, the global average sea surface temperature recorded 20.97 degrees Celsius, and due to the El Niño phenomenon, there is an increasing possibility of record-breaking heatwaves next year [25, 154]. In Kabul, Afghanistan, a desperate water shortage crisis is emerging due to the dual challenges of war and drought [31], and disruptions in global coffee production caused by climate change are leading to supply pressures [169]. Ski industry experts have also expressed deep concerns about the impact of climate change on their activities [160]. A Gallup poll indicating that concerns about climate change in the U.S. are nearing an all-time high [156] demonstrates a rising global awareness of the climate crisis. Environmental activists are urging urgent action to address climate change [173, 174], and criticisms are also being raised regarding the slow pace of response from some local communities and coalition parties [172].

sections.title: 🌍 International Cooperation, Climate Technology, and the Future
sections.content: International cooperation and technological innovation for climate crisis response are continuously being pursued. A UN Climate Change Convention meeting will be held in Korea for the first time in three years, where discussions on climate technology transfer and dissemination are planned [4]. The Ministry of Science and ICT is strengthening cooperation with the Global Green Growth Institute (GGGI) to address climate change [5, 22]. Hungary's new prime minister has expressed a positive stance on climate change response, anticipating strengthened environmental regulations [6]. The Institute of Food Technologists (IFT) has published a new white paper on improving food and nutrition security through climate change mitigation and adaptation [171]. Climate action is spreading across various sectors of society, with youth in Montana, for example, advocating for the allowance of climate change lawsuits [159]. The case of the University of the South Pacific (USP) awarding climate change scholarships [175] demonstrates that international efforts to cultivate climate change experts are also ongoing.

briefing.closing: Through the climate-related news covered in today's briefing, we have confirmed that climate change response efforts are actively underway both domestically and internationally. Simultaneously, we acknowledge the warnings of the intensifying climate crisis and the challenges we face, and we once again emphasize the need for more swift and systematic responses. This concludes today's climate briefing.
